bio.title: Pham Tuan Anh
bio.desc: Former game developer turned data analyst. Building end-to-end pipelines and uncovering insights in real-world datasets.

p1.title: Global Tech Recruit Analysis
p1.desc: End-to-end pipeline — 1.6M job postings cleaned, 12 visualisations, salary regression model R² 0.35.

p2.title: Cookie Cats A/B Testing
p2.desc: Analysed 90K players to measure the impact of gate placement on retention. Bootstrap testing confirms statistical significance.

p3.title: E-Commerce Segmentation
p3.desc: Segmented 4,372 customers using RFM analysis on 542K transactions. Built cohort retention heatmap and business recommendations.

footer.data: Data sourced from Google Jobs, Cookie Cats (Kaggle), UCI Online Retail II

bio.intro: Data analyst with a background in game development. I build end-to-end data pipelines, run statistical analyses, and turn messy datasets into clear, actionable insights. Currently looking for opportunities to grow in a data-driven team.
bio.s1_sub: Tools and technologies I work with
bio.pivot_text: Before data, I shipped 2 games on Google Play. That experience taught me systems thinking, performance optimisation, and iterating under constraints — skills that transfer directly to data analysis.

p1.sub: End-to-end data pipeline connecting MotherDuck cloud warehouse via DuckDB, cleaning 1.6M job postings, producing 12 Plotly visualisations, and training a salary regression model — R² 0.35 on US market data.
p1.s1_sub: Median annual salary by job title — US market, 2023–2025
p1.s2_sub: Monthly job postings volume and remote-work share, Jan 2023 – Nov 2024
p1.s3_sub: Top in-demand skills and leading countries by posting volume
p1.s1_insight: Senior ML and Data Science roles command the highest premiums — exceeding $140K median in the US. Roles offering remote flexibility correlate with above-average compensation. Recommendation: target senior-level data roles with remote options for maximum earning potential.
p1.s2_insight: Hiring volume peaked in early 2023 and normalised through 2024, reflecting post-pandemic recalibration. Remote work share held stable at 20–30% — data roles retain stronger remote negotiability. Recommendation: leverage remote-friendly positioning when applying for data roles.
p1.s3_insight: SQL is the universal language of data — appearing in 385K postings, it outpaces Python by roughly 2×. The US dominates, but India and the UK represent fast-growing demand centres. Recommendation: prioritise SQL and Python proficiency; consider distributed teams for broader opportunities.
p1.s4_sub: Gradient-boosted classifier — high vs low pay, US jobs
p1.chart_features_sub: Feature importance · green = higher pay, red = lower
p1.s4_insight: Whether a posting beats the median salary is highly predictable: the classifier reaches 80% accuracy (ROC-AUC 0.85) against a 50% coin-flip baseline. Seniority and role do most of the work, but among skills, distributed-compute and cloud (Spark, AWS/Azure) are the strongest signals of high pay, while spreadsheet and BI tools (Excel, Power BI) point the other way. Recommendation: to reach the upper salary band, pair a senior or engineering track with cloud and big-data skills.
p1.ml_note: How it works: Each posting is labelled high pay if its salary exceeds the dataset median ($110K), giving two balanced classes. A gradient-boosting classifier is trained on role, seniority, country and the 10 skills (one-hot encoded), then scored on a held-out test set. Bars show each feature's importance — its share of total predictive weight; green pushes a posting toward high pay, red toward low pay.
p1.s5_sub: Data-driven moves to maximise earning potential
recs_items: Build on a SQL + Python foundation — they appear in the most postings and underpin nearly every higher-paying role.
Invest in distributed-compute and cloud skills (Spark, AWS, Azure): they carry the largest salary premiums and most strongly separate high- from low-paying postings.
Aim for senior or engineering tracks — seniority and an engineering title are the strongest predictors of landing above the median salary.
Target remote-friendly, US-based roles where feasible: they concentrate the highest compensation in the dataset.

p2.sub: Analysed an A/B test from the mobile game Cookie Cats (90,189 players) to determine whether moving the first gate from level 30 to level 40 affects player retention. Applied chi-square tests and bootstrap analysis to validate results.
p2.s1_sub: Random assignment of players into two groups
p2.s2_sub: 1-day and 7-day retention rates by gate position
p2.s3_sub: 1,000 bootstrap iterations — distribution of retention difference
p2.s4_sub: Total rounds played per group — player engagement patterns
p2.s1_insight: The experiment has a well-balanced design with 44,700 players in Gate 30 and 45,489 in Gate 40. Random assignment ensures no selection bias, making the groups directly comparable.
p2.s2_insight: Gate 30 outperforms Gate 40 on both retention metrics: 44.82% vs 44.23% (1-day) and 19.02% vs 18.20% (7-day). The 7-day difference is statistically significant (p=0.0016), while the 1-day difference is not (p=0.076). Recommendation: keeping the gate at level 30 preserves higher long-term retention.
p2.s3_insight: The entire bootstrap distribution sits above zero (95% CI: [0.34%, 1.28%]), confirming that Gate 30 consistently produces higher 7-day retention than Gate 40. The mean difference of 0.83 percentage points represents a meaningful lift for a game with millions of players.
p2.note_retention: Reading these bars: each is the return rate within its own group (players who came back ÷ that group's size), so the two don't add to 100% — they're independent rates being compared, not a shared split. Whether the gap is real is tested with a chi-square test on the underlying player counts (a 2×2 table, df = 1): day-1 p = 0.0755 (not significant — a gap this size occurs ~7.5% of the time by chance), day-7 p = 0.0016 (significant — under ~0.2%).
p2.note_bootstrap: What the bootstrap does: the Gate 30 − Gate 40 difference is recomputed on thousands of resamples of the players (drawn with replacement), producing the distribution above. The 95% confidence interval [+0.34, +1.28 pp] is simply the middle 95% of that cloud. Because the whole interval sits to the right of the dashed zero line, the day-7 advantage holds up under resampling — it's consistent, not an artefact of one random split.
p2.s4_insight: Both groups show similar engagement patterns, with the majority of players completing 1–10 rounds. The gate placement does not appear to significantly alter overall play behaviour, but it does affect whether players return after their first session.
p2.conclusion_text: Keep the gate at level 30. The data shows a statistically significant improvement in 7-day retention (p=0.0016) when the gate is placed at level 30 versus level 40. While 1-day retention shows a similar trend, the difference is not significant at the 5% level. The bootstrap analysis reinforces this conclusion — the entire 95% confidence interval for the retention difference is positive. Moving the gate to level 40 would reduce long-term player retention with no compensating engagement benefit.

p3.sub: Analysed 541,909 transactions from a UK-based online retailer to segment 4,372 customers using RFM analysis, build cohort retention heatmaps, and generate actionable business recommendations for improving customer lifetime value.
p3.s1_sub: Monthly revenue trend and geographic distribution
p3.s2_sub: Customer segments based on Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value
p3.s3_sub: Monthly cohort retention rates — tracking customer return behaviour
p3.chart_cohort_sub: % of customers returning each month after first purchase
p3.s4_sub: Data-driven strategies to improve customer retention and revenue
p3.s1_insight: Revenue shows clear seasonality, peaking in Sep–Nov (Q4 holiday season) with November reaching $1.46M. The UK dominates at 82% of total revenue, suggesting high geographic concentration risk. Recommendation: diversify marketing to high-potential markets like Netherlands and Germany.
p3.s2_insight: Champions (632 customers, 14.5%) drive disproportionate revenue at $6,820 avg vs $310 for Lost customers. The largest segment is Hibernating (1,040 customers), representing a significant win-back opportunity. Recommendation: focus retention spend on At-Risk customers before they become Lost.
p3.s3_insight: The Dec 2010 cohort shows the strongest retention (36–41% returning monthly), likely due to holiday acquisition of high-intent buyers. Later cohorts show declining retention, suggesting acquisition quality dropped. Recommendation: replicate holiday acquisition tactics year-round to capture higher-quality customers.
recs_items: Launch a loyalty programme for Champions (14.5% of customers, highest avg revenue of $6,820) to maintain engagement and increase cross-sell opportunities.
Create a win-back email campaign targeting At-Risk customers (593) with personalised offers before they lapse into the Hibernating or Lost segments.
Diversify geographic revenue — Netherlands and Germany show strong potential but represent only 3.2% and 2.6% of revenue respectively. Invest in localised marketing.
Replicate Q4 acquisition tactics — the Dec 2010 cohort retained at 36–41% monthly, significantly above later cohorts. Analyse what made these customers different.
